systemPrompt:
You are a helpful assistant for the Synfinii University Training Dashboard — an interactive learning application built for Synfinii users. You can answer questions both about the Synfinii platform itself and about how to use this learning application.

ABOUT THE LEARNING APPLICATION:
- The dashboard has five navigation views: Overview, Modules, Assistant, Learning Paths, and Reports (admin only).
- Overview shows overall progress, per-track completion, and an "Up next" prompt.
- Modules lets users browse and work through learning modules organized into four role tracks.
- Learning Paths shows a decision tree to help users choose the right track, plus descriptions of all tracks.
- Assistant is this AI chat interface.
- Reports is only visible when signed in as the admin account and shows all user progress plus CSV export options.

ACCOUNTS & SIGN-IN:
- Users create an account with a username and a 4-digit PIN. Returning users sign in with the same credentials.
- Sessions persist — returning to the dashboard on the same device will auto sign you in.
- To sign out, click the "Sign out" button in the top-right header.
- The special username "admin" unlocks the Reports tab for viewing all learner progress.
- First-time users are shown the Learning Paths screen automatically on first sign-in.

HOW MODULES WORK:
- Modules unlock sequentially — you must complete the previous module before the next one becomes available.
- Each module follows a flow: Watch video (if required) → Read all lessons → Take the quiz → Complete activity (if present).
- Videos must be confirmed watched before lessons unlock. All lessons must be read before the quiz unlocks. The quiz must be completed before the activity unlocks.
- To mark a lesson read, click "Mark as read" at the bottom of each lesson.
- The quiz requires you to select an answer and click "Check answer" for each question. Once all questions are revealed, click "Mark module complete" or "Continue to activity" if an activity is present.
- Activities require a 70% score to pass. Failed activities can be retried immediately.
- Activity types include: scenario-based decisions, drag-and-drop categorisation, and fill-in-the-blank.
- Completed modules show a green checkmark. Locked modules are grayed out.

PROGRESS & EXPORT:
- Progress is saved automatically to your account and persists across sessions and devices.
- In the Overview tab, click "Download my progress" to export your own completion data as a CSV.
- As admin, use the Reports tab to see all users and export Summary or Detailed CSVs. The detailed CSV includes activity scores.
- Exports open as a copy-to-clipboard modal — copy the text, paste into Notepad or TextEdit, save as .csv, and open in Excel.

FOUR ROLE TRACKS:
- Data Steward (DS): 6 modules covering platform governance, entity management, data quality, rules, and audit.
- Resolution Services (RS): 5 modules covering trade processing, the resolution queue, entity matching, hierarchy resolution, and promotion.
- Sales Reporting (SR): 5 modules covering ThoughtSpot dashboards, segmentation, report building, validation, and scheduling.
- Administrator (AD): 6 modules covering platform architecture, RBAC/security, feed management, rules engine, monitoring, and audit readiness.

SYNFINII PLATFORM CONCEPTS:
AUM (Assets Under Management), Flows (purchases minus redemptions), Entity Management (firms/offices/advisors hierarchy), Matching & Apply system (resolves candidate records against MASTER records using field weights, thresholds, and deterministic rules to produce authoritative master records), Resolution Queue / Resolution Cockpit (unmatched trades needing manual attribution — Operations > Resolution; has a 'Resolved Today' tab and threshold filtering), Smart Suggestions (automated match candidates in the cockpit using fuzzy, exact alias, pattern, and CCOP matching strategies), CLIENT_NOT_FOUND resolution modes (Create Alias = permanent mapping; Reassign Alias = one-time override; Default to House = route to office house account), ThoughtSpot (AI search analytics — Live Boards with multi-level drill-down, Spotter natural language AI search, AI Highlights for automated insight surfacing, threshold alerts, TML for cross-org model deployment), SOX ITGCs (Change Management, Access Control, System Availability), three environments (Dev→UAT→Production), four roles (Data Steward owns data, Resolution Services resolves orphan trades, Sales Reporting builds reports, Administrator owns system config and ThoughtSpot data workspace), 10-stage financial processing pipeline plus Stage 0 PRE_INIT (Stage 0 pre-init exclusion → Stage 1 init → Stage 2 source validation → Stage 3 product ID → Stage 4 market product ID → Stage 5 transaction classification → Stage 6 currency → Stage 7 valuation → Stage 8 alias remapping → Stage 9 client attribution → Stage 10 business rules), Workspace/Release system (Workspaces are transient dev environments; Releases are versioned with Type 2 SCD; REPLACE strategy replaces all rules; AUGMENT strategy merges rules; two-level priority: Release priority controls order between releases, rule priority controls order within a release).

Be encouraging, clear, and concise — under 200 words per response.

ds.label: Data Steward
ds.desc: Own and curate mastered data. Monitor quality, resolve exceptions, and enforce rules.

modules.title: Platform & Governance Foundation
modules.desc: Navigate Synfinii's key data domains and understand the data flow architecture.

lessons.title: What is Synfinii?
lessons.content:
Synfinii is a data management, reporting, and analytics platform purpose-built for asset managers. Its tagline — 'Synthesize Data. Activate Outcomes.' — describes its core mission: take fragmented, conflicting data from multiple source systems and produce a single, high-integrity master record that every team can trust.

Without Synfinii, data about the same advisor might exist in three different systems with three different values. Synfinii resolves those conflicts into one authoritative answer — the 'Source of Truth.'

lessons.title: The five core responsibilities
lessons.content:
Synfinii serves five functions:

1. Data Integrity & Source of Truth — reconciling conflicting data from Salesforce, SalesConnect, Broadridge, and third-party providers into a single master record.

2. Complex Financial Attribution — automatically mapping trades and assets to the correct advisors and firms.

3. Regulatory Compliance & Auditability — maintaining a transparent audit trail of every data decision for SEC/FINRA reviews.

4. Self-Service Business Intelligence — AI-powered search analytics via ThoughtSpot so teams can query data without IT help.

5. Seamless System Interoperability — feeding clean, mastered data to downstream systems like SalesPage for compensation and CRM.

lessons.title: The three data domains
lessons.content:
As a Data Steward, you'll work across three interconnected domains:

Entity — the 'Who.' Firms, offices, and individual advisors. Every financial transaction must be linked to a valid entity record to be reported on.

Financial — the 'How Much.' Trades, AUM (Assets Under Management), and flows (money moving in and out of products). Financial data is only meaningful when correctly attributed to the right entity.

Product — the 'What.' Mutual funds, ETFs, share classes, and other investment vehicles. Each product has identifiers (CUSIPs, ISINs, tickers) that link financial transactions to the correct investment.

lessons.title: The data flow architecture
lessons.content:
Data in Synfinii follows a structured pipeline:

Ingest → The system receives raw files from providers via FTP, S3, API, or manual upload. Every raw record is archived immediately — this is your audit safety net.

Validate → The data passes through a scrubbing layer that checks for formatting errors, missing identifiers, and invalid values.

Master → The matching and Apply system reconciles data from multiple sources, resolves conflicts, and produces the master record.

Promote → Clean, mastered data flows downstream to reporting tables and to SalesPage for compensation and CRM.

quiz.q: Synfinii describes itself as the 'Source of Truth.' What does this mean in practice?
opts: It is the only system allowed to store data
It reconciles data from multiple sources into a single validated master record every department can trust
It automatically deletes duplicates from all connected systems
It generates financial reports without human input
quiz.exp: Synfinii reconciles fragmented data from sources like Salesforce, SalesConnect, and third-party providers into one high-integrity master record.

quiz.q: Which best describes the data flow architecture in Synfinii?
opts: Data enters once and is never updated
Raw data is ingested, validated, mastered, and then promoted to downstream systems
Data flows directly from SalesPage into Synfinii
All data is manually entered by Data Stewards
quiz.exp: Synfinii ingests raw data, validates it, masters it through the matching and Apply system, and promotes clean data downstream.

quiz.q: What are the three key data domains a Data Steward must understand?
opts: Sales, Marketing, Finance
Entity, Financial, and Product
Import, Export, and Archive
Advisor, Client, and Territory
quiz.exp: Entity (firms, offices, advisors), Financial (trades, AUM, flows), and Product (mutual funds, ETFs, share classes) are the three core domains.

modules.title: Entity Master Data Management
modules.desc: Create and maintain client entity records with accuracy and completeness.

lessons.title: What is an entity in Synfinii?
lessons.content:
An entity is any real-world party that interacts with investment products — a firm, an office, or an individual advisor. Synfinii maintains a three-level hierarchy:

National Account (Grandparent) → e.g. Merrill Lynch nationwide
Regional Office (Parent) → e.g. Merrill Lynch New York
Individual Advisor (Child) → a specific registered rep

This hierarchy enables roll-up reporting — a sale at the New York office correctly attributes up to the national Merrill Lynch account.

lessons.title: Creating a new entity record
lessons.content:
When creating a new advisor record, you must capture:

Mandatory identifiers — CRD number, Rep Code, and Tax ID. These are the 'keys' used to match incoming trades to this advisor. Missing any one of them will cause attribution failures.

Hierarchy link — which office does this advisor belong to? Without this link, roll-up reporting breaks.

Status — is this advisor active, inactive, or terminated?

All mandatory fields must be populated before the record can be promoted to the master tables.

lessons.title: Soft matches and deduplication
lessons.content:
When a new record enters the system, Synfinii's matching algorithm compares it against existing records:

Hard match (auto-link) — the similarity score is at or above the auto-match threshold (default: 90). Records are automatically linked to the existing master.

Soft match (review queue) — the score falls between the potential match threshold (default: 60) and the auto-match threshold. These are flagged for Data Steward review.

Your job: review the evidence and decide — merge or keep separate. Every merge decision is logged in the audit trail with your name and timestamp.

lessons.title: Cross-referencing identifiers
lessons.content:
Synfinii's identifier cross-referencing is like a 'Rosetta Stone' — it maintains an alias table so that when Broadridge sends a trade with one identifier and SalesPage expects a different one, the system bridges them automatically.

Aliases are source-specific: the same external code can map to different advisors depending on which data provider sent it. Identifiers include: CRD number, NPN, Internal IDs from Salesforce or SalesPage, and source-specific Rep Codes. You maintain the accuracy of this alias library as part of your Data Steward role.

quiz.q: When creating a new advisor record, which field is most critical for linking that advisor to financial transactions?
opts: Email address
Office location
Industry identifier (e.g. CRD or Rep Code)
Date of hire
quiz.exp: CRD numbers and Rep Codes are the primary keys used to match incoming financial transactions to the correct advisor.

quiz.q: A new firm has branches in three cities. How should this be structured in Synfinii?
opts: One record with all three cities
A parent record for the national firm with child records for each branch
Three separate unlinked records
Only the headquarters
quiz.exp: Parent-child hierarchy enables roll-up reporting. Sales at a local branch correctly attribute up to the national account.

quiz.q: What is a 'soft match' in Synfinii?
opts: A permanently merged record
A record that scores between the potential match and auto-match thresholds, requiring human review
A record from a low-quality source
An advisor who hasn't logged in recently
quiz.exp: Soft matches are potential duplicates requiring a Data Steward to review and decide — merge or keep separate.

modules.title: Data Quality Monitoring & Triage
modules.desc: Use the Data Quality Dashboard to identify, prioritize, and triage exceptions.

lessons.title: The Data Quality Dashboard
lessons.content: The Data Quality Dashboard is your primary monitoring tool. It surfaces exceptions — records that fail completeness, consistency, validity, or timeliness checks — and lets you filter, prioritize, and assign them for resolution. Your daily routine as a Data Steward typically starts here.

lessons.title: Triage: prioritizing what matters most
lessons.content:
Priority 1 — Active high-volume advisors with missing identifiers. Their trades can't be attributed, directly impacting AUM reporting and compensation.

Priority 2 — Broken hierarchy links. These cause roll-up reporting failures at the national account level.

Priority 3 — Duplicate records on active entities. These inflate headcount and can double-count AUM.

Priority 4 — Formatting issues on low-activity or inactive records. Real but low-urgency.

lessons.title: Detecting patterns in exceptions
lessons.content:
A spike in exceptions from a single source feed usually means something changed upstream — the provider altered their file format, sent incomplete data, or introduced a new code the system doesn't recognize.

Always ask: is this one record's problem, or is this 50 records' problem with the same root cause? The answer determines whether you fix one record or escalate a systematic fix.

lessons.title: Using filters and search
lessons.content:
Filter by source — isolate all exceptions from a specific feed
Filter by error type — group by missing Tax ID, duplicate record, invalid identifier
Filter by entity type — focus on firms, offices, or advisors separately
Filter by severity — show only P1 and P2 issues
Sort by date — see most recent exceptions first
Search by entity name or identifier — jump to a specific firm or advisor

quiz.q: You open the Data Quality Dashboard and see 200 exceptions. What is your first step?
opts: Resolve all 200 immediately
Filter and prioritize by severity, source, and business impact before resolving
Escalate all 200 to IT immediately
Ignore them until the number exceeds 500
quiz.exp: Effective triage means filtering by severity and impact first.

quiz.q: The dashboard shows a spike in exceptions from a specific source feed. What does this likely indicate?
opts: Spikes are normal — no action needed
A change in the source feed's format or data quality that needs investigation
All records from that source should be deleted
The feed has been permanently disconnected
quiz.exp: A sudden spike from one source usually means something changed upstream.

quiz.q: Which exception type would you prioritize most urgently?
opts: Minor formatting inconsistency on an archived record
Missing Tax ID on an active high-volume advisor whose trades are currently unattributed
Outdated phone number on a low-activity account
Duplicate record for a firm that closed two years ago
quiz.exp: Missing Tax IDs on active advisors directly impact financial attribution.

activities.title: Exception triage prioritisation
activities.intro: Your Data Quality Dashboard has just loaded with the following exceptions. Assign each one to the correct priority level based on what you learned about triage.

items.label: Missing Tax ID on an active advisor processing $2M in weekly trades
Broken parent-child link on a national Merrill Lynch account
Duplicate record found for an active firm with 50 linked advisors
Phone number formatted incorrectly on an account closed 18 months ago
Missing CRD number on a high-volume advisor — trades currently unattributed
Office hierarchy link broken — roll-up reporting failing for a regional account

modules.title: Data Quality Resolution
modules.desc: Merge duplicates, correct invalid attributes, and override soft matches.

lessons.title: The three resolution actions
lessons.content:
1. Correction — directly update a field value in the record. The correction updates the master record and is logged with your name, timestamp, and before/after values.

2. Merge — combine two records that represent the same entity. One record becomes the 'winner' (its data is retained) and one is absorbed. All historical transactions linked to the absorbed record are re-attributed to the winner.

3. Override — temporarily suppress a matching or Apply decision for a specific record without changing the underlying configuration.

lessons.title: Merging records: choosing the winner
lessons.content:
Before merging, ask:
— Which record has more complete data?
— Which has higher-authority identifiers (CRD vs. a source-specific code)?
— Which has more linked transactions?
— Which was created from a more reliable source?

Remember: you can un-merge. If you merge incorrectly, the un-merge function restores both records to their pre-merge state. Use it promptly.

lessons.title: Corrections vs. overrides
lessons.content:
A correction changes the actual data in a record permanently. Use when a value is genuinely wrong — a mis-keyed Tax ID, a name spelled incorrectly.

An override suppresses a matching or Apply decision for a specific record without touching the underlying data or configuration. Use when the system's automated decision is correct in general but wrong for this specific case.

lessons.title: Audit trail discipline
lessons.content:
Every resolution action you take generates an immutable audit log entry recording what changed, who made the change, when, and why.

Best practice: always enter a brief reason with your resolution actions. 'Merged per CRD cross-reference — confirmed same advisor at two offices' is far more defensible than a blank reason field.

quiz.q: Before merging two advisor records, what must you verify?
opts: Both records have identical email addresses
Which record is the 'winner' — the one whose data will be retained in the master
That the advisor has consented to the merge
Both records were created on the same date
quiz.exp: Choosing the wrong winner corrupts the master record. Review data quality, source authority, and completeness before deciding.

quiz.q: After merging, a colleague confirms the advisors are actually different people. What do you do?
opts: It's irreversible — nothing can be done
Submit a service request to IT to delete both records
Use the un-merge function to restore both records to their pre-merge state
Create a brand new record for one advisor
quiz.exp: Synfinii's un-merge function reverses an incorrect merge and restores both records.

quiz.q: What is the key difference between a correction and an override?
opts: They are the same action
A correction updates the data permanently; an override suppresses a system decision without changing the underlying data or configuration
An override is permanent; a correction is temporary
Only IT Administrators can make corrections
quiz.exp: Overrides redirect automated decisions for specific cases without touching underlying data or configuration. Corrections update the actual data.

modules.title: Mastering Rules & Configuration
modules.desc: Understand how matching and Apply configurations work and when to escalate changes.

lessons.title: How the matching and Apply system works
lessons.content:
Synfinii resolves conflicts and creates authoritative master records through a two-step process: Matching and Apply.

Matching — when candidate records arrive from a source, the matching engine compares them against existing MASTER records using configurable match configurations. Each configuration defines:
— Field weights: how much each field (name, address, financial identifier, email) contributes to a similarity score
— Thresholds: scores at or above the auto-match threshold (default: 90) auto-link; scores between the potential match threshold (default: 60) and 90 go to the manual review queue; scores below 60 are treated as new records
— Deterministic rules: trusted identifiers (like a CRD number) that produce an automatic 100% match when present
— Blocking rules: reduce the search space before scoring (e.g., only compare FIRM candidates to FIRM masters)

Apply — once matching is complete, the Apply configuration controls what happens to the MASTER record:
— Which fields get updated from the candidate
— Which fields are protected and never overwritten
— Source priority: when multiple candidates from different sources conflict, the source with the highest priority 'wins' for that field
— Whether changes require human approval through the Apply Review queue

The result is a MASTER record — the single authoritative version with each field populated by the most trusted, most current value available.

lessons.title: Rule types and their impact
lessons.content:
Business rules in Synfinii inject custom logic at specific points in the data processing lifecycle without requiring code changes. Rules are authored in Workspaces and deployed to production through Releases.

Each rule has three components:

Binding — specifies where and when the rule executes: a Domain Object (e.g., financial_candidate, profile) and a Hook Point (the lifecycle moment). The most common hook point is FINANCIAL_STAGE_10_FINAL_RULES (after all 9 earlier pipeline stages are complete). Rules can also fire at FINANCIAL_STAGE_0_PRE_INIT (before processing begins — to filter or exclude records) and on PRE_CREATE / POST_CREATE lifecycle events for entities.

Conditions — determine which records within a batch the rule fires on. Conditions use AND logic. Types include: Audience Selection (JSON Logic expressions like 'gross_amount > 1,000,000'), Pattern Selection (regex matching), Record Selection (batch lookup against another table), and Watchlist Membership.

Actions — execute on all records that passed conditions. Types include: Set Field (update a field value using a constant, template, or field copy), Create Work Item (route for human review with configurable priority), Watchlist Action (add/remove from monitoring lists), Service Action (create or update records in a related table), and Exclude Candidate (only at PRE_INIT — permanently excludes matching candidates from all further processing).

Two-level priority system: Rules execute in a specific order controlled at two levels. First, Release priority determines which Release runs first when multiple Releases are active (e.g., a 'Hotfixes' Release with priority 100 executes before 'Base Rules' with priority 0). Second, within each Release, each rule has its own priority that controls the order within that Release. This composable system lets teams layer rule sets — a hotfix Release can sit on top of a base Release without replacing it.

As a Data Steward, you identify when rules need to change — the Administrator implements the change through the Workspace/Release system.

lessons.title: Identifying when a rule or configuration needs to change
lessons.content:
Signs that a matching or Apply configuration needs review:
— The same type of error recurs repeatedly from the same source
— A source now consistently produces wrong values in a specific field
— A new source hasn't been calibrated yet
— Match scores are consistently near the threshold, causing too many manual reviews
— A business change means existing logic no longer applies

Signs that a business rule needs review:
— The same type of processing exception recurs repeatedly
— Records that should be flagged are passing through without review
— Records that should not be flagged are generating unnecessary work items
— A new data source introduces patterns the existing rules don't handle

Document clearly: what the current behavior produces, what it should produce, how many records are affected, and the business impact. Escalate to the IT Administrator to implement changes.

lessons.title: The exception queue as feedback
lessons.content:
Think of the Resolution Cockpit not just as a task list but as a feedback mechanism. Every error group tells you something about the health of your data pipeline.

High volume of PRODUCT_NOT_FOUND for a specific source → a fund code or CUSIP may have changed with the provider; add the new alias to the existing product record

High volume of CLIENT_NOT_FOUND for a new source → client aliases haven't been set up for this source code yet; the initial alias-building period is normal

Sudden spike in FIRM_NOT_FOUND after a nightly cycle → a provider may have changed their firm codes; check if the source code's executing_at setting is appropriate

Recurring TRANSACTION_CODE_NOT_FOUND for specific codes → provider added new transaction types; create source-specific transaction code mappings

A sudden increase in any error type after a nightly cycle → something in that cycle's source file format or content may have changed; check the ingestion logs

quiz.q: The matching system uses 'field weights.' What determines how much weight a field carries?
opts: How recently it was updated
How reliably and distinctively that field identifies a unique entity — configured per match setup
The number of records that contain it
Whether it is a mandatory field
quiz.exp: Field weights are configured in the match configuration and reflect how much each field (name, address, financial identifier, email) contributes to the overall similarity score.

quiz.q: You notice a matching configuration consistently produces incorrect auto-links. What is the correct course of action?
opts: Manually fix each affected record
Document the pattern, escalate a configuration change request to the IT Administrator, and continue monitoring
Delete all affected records
Disable the matching configuration entirely
quiz.exp: Data Stewards identify matching and rule problems; IT Administrators implement configuration changes.

quiz.q: When the matching engine cannot reach the auto-match threshold, what happens?
opts: The system randomly selects one value
The most recent value always wins
Records scoring above the potential match threshold go to the Match Review Queue for Data Steward review
The field is left blank
quiz.exp: Records scoring between the potential match and auto-match thresholds go to the review queue. Data Stewards review, make the call, and their decisions are logged.

modules.title: Audit & Data Lineage Tracking
modules.desc: Use the audit log to trace data changes for compliance and troubleshooting.

lessons.title: The audit trail in Synfinii
lessons.content: Synfinii maintains a permanent, immutable audit trail of every data change. It records what changed, who changed it, when, how, and why. As a Data Steward, you both contribute to this trail and query it when you need to answer 'why did this data change?' questions.

lessons.title: Data lineage: tracing a value's journey
lessons.content:
Data lineage is the traceable history of a data point — from its original source system, through every transformation and decision, to its current state in the master record.

Example: An address field shows '456 Oak Avenue.' Lineage lets you trace: which source sent it, when, which source 'won' the candidate battle through the Apply process, and whether any manual overrides were applied.

lessons.title: When you'll need the audit log
lessons.content:
Troubleshooting — 'This advisor's address changed and trades aren't matching. What happened?'

Compliance review — 'Show me all changes made to advisor records in Q3.'

Escalation support — When you escalate a configuration change request, the audit trail showing the pattern of incorrect decisions over time is your strongest supporting evidence.

lessons.title: Compliance verification using lineage
lessons.content:
SOX requires that you can demonstrate:
— Data changes were made by authorized personnel only
— Changes followed a documented and approved process
— No unauthorized modifications were made to financial data
— A complete, unbroken chain of custody exists for all master records

Your audit trail discipline — always entering reasons, following the resolution SOP — directly supports the firm's ability to pass SOX audits.

quiz.q: A regulator asks why the address for Firm X changed on March 15. Where do you find this answer?
opts: The data import log
The audit trail — which records who changed it, what it was before, what it is now, and when
The resolution queue history
You would need to contact the data provider
quiz.exp: Synfinii's audit trail records every data change with full before/after values, timestamps, and the user or system responsible.

quiz.q: What is 'data lineage' and why does it matter for compliance?
opts: The age of a data record
The traceable history of a data point from its original source through every transformation to its current state
The process of importing data from providers
A measure of data completeness
quiz.exp: Data lineage shows the full journey of a data point. For SOX compliance, this traceable history is the evidence that data decisions were made transparently.

quiz.q: During an audit, you're asked to prove only authorized users accessed compensation data in Q4. What provides this evidence?
opts: The data quality dashboard
The access and audit logs showing user activity, timestamps, and data accessed
The resolution queue
The nightly cycle report
quiz.exp: Access logs are a core SOX IT General Control deliverable.

rs.label: Resolution Services
rs.desc: Reconcile unmatched trades, assets, and money flows to the correct master record.

modules.title: Transactional Data Flow & Error Types
modules.desc: Understand how trade data flows into Synfinii and what causes attribution failures.

lessons.title: The financial processing pipeline
lessons.content:
Every trade passes through a structured 10-stage processing pipeline before it can be reported on. There is also a Stage 0 that runs before the pipeline begins:

Stage 0: Pre-Initialization Rules — rules that can exclude a candidate entirely before any processing begins (no error queue entry, no fact record).
Stage 1: Initialization — prepares the processing batch, adds output columns.
Stage 2: Source Code Validation — verifies the source code is configured.
Stage 3: Product Identification — matches CUSIP, ticker, or fund code to a product class (for trade and account candidates).
Stage 4: Market Product Identification — matches Morningstar category or portfolio classification to a market product (for market flow and market position candidates).
Stage 5: Transaction Classification — maps the transaction code to a trade type and trade class.
Stage 6: Currency Resolution — ensures every record has a resolved currency, defaulting to USD.
Stage 7: Valuation — calculates gross amounts using product prices and FX rates.
Stage 8: Alias Remapping — normalises firm, office, and qualifier codes before client attribution.
Stage 9: Client Attribution — resolves the Firm → Office → Contact hierarchy.
Stage 10: Business Rules — applies any custom processing logic from the active release.

If any stage fails, the trade enters the Resolution Queue with a specific error code identifying exactly where it stopped.

lessons.title: Common error types
lessons.content:
The financial processing pipeline generates specific error codes when a record cannot be processed. These are the exact error codes you will see in the Resolution Cockpit:

PRODUCT_NOT_FOUND — the product identifiers (CUSIP, ticker, or fund code) don't match any product in the master. This is the most common reference data error.

MARKET_PRODUCT_NOT_FOUND — for market flow and market position candidates, the Morningstar category or portfolio classification couldn't be matched to a known market product.

TRANSACTION_CODE_NOT_FOUND — the transaction code in the file isn't mapped to a trade type and trade class for this source.

SOURCE_CODE_NOT_FOUND — the source code on the candidate doesn't match any configured source code. This is an administrative configuration error — no resolution is available from the cockpit.

CLIENT_NOT_FOUND — the rep/contact code couldn't be matched to a client profile. The most complex error to resolve, with three available resolution modes: Create Alias, Reassign Alias, and Default to House.

FIRM_NOT_FOUND — the firm or office codes couldn't be matched to a firm profile. Also generated for office-level failures when the source is configured with executing_at = 'OFFICE'. OFFICE_NOT_FOUND as a standalone code is deprecated.

ACCOUNT_NOT_FOUND — the account number wasn't recognized.

ACCOUNTCLIENT_NOT_FOUND — the account exists but has no account-to-client relationship.

PRODUCT_PRICE_NOT_FOUND — no NAV or price record exists for the product on the trade date.

CURRENCY_CODE_NOT_FOUND — the currency code doesn't exist in the currency table.

CURRENCY_VALUE_NOT_FOUND / FX_RATE_NOT_FOUND — no exchange rate exists for the currency pair on the required date. These refer to the same condition.

lessons.title: Why raw archiving matters
lessons.content:
Before processing begins, Synfinii archives every incoming record in its raw, unmodified form.

Audit safety net — if a question arises about what came in from a source, the raw archive settles it definitively.

Error investigation — the raw archive shows exactly what the source sent, helping you understand whether the error is in Synfinii's configuration or in the source file.

Think of the raw archive as the 'before' photo. It never changes.

lessons.title: Your role in the pipeline
lessons.content:
You are the human checkpoint in Synfinii's automated pipeline. Your core responsibilities:
— Investigate why a trade failed to process
— Research the correct attribution using Synfinii's lookup tools
— Manually link the trade to the correct entity
— Promote the resolved trade back into the pipeline
— Escalate to the Data Steward when the root cause is a missing master record
— Document your resolution with a clear reason

Key navigation for your daily work:
Financial > Candidates > Resolution Queue — your primary workspace
Financial > Candidates > Candidates — browse all candidates and their processing status
Financial > Transactions — confirm resolved trades appear as fact records
Financial > Products > Classes — check the product master when resolving PRODUCT_NOT_FOUND
Clients menu — look up firms, offices, and contacts when resolving client errors

quiz.q: A trade enters Synfinii. What is the first thing the system tries to do?
opts: Immediately promote it to the master reporting tables
Validate it against known source codes, product identifiers, and transaction types before processing
Send it to a Data Steward for manual review
Export it to SalesPage
quiz.exp: Every incoming record passes through a 10-stage validation pipeline first.

quiz.q: What does CLIENT_NOT_FOUND mean as an error code?
opts: The advisor's account has been deactivated
The trade's rep identifier couldn't be matched to any client profile in the master data
The rep submitted the trade incorrectly
The rep's name is spelled differently in two systems
quiz.exp: CLIENT_NOT_FOUND means the trade came in with a rep/contact code that doesn't correspond to any known advisor alias.

quiz.q: Why does Synfinii archive every trade in its raw form before processing?
opts: To slow processing for quality control
To create an immutable record of what was received — the audit safety net for regulatory reviews
Because processed versions take too much storage
It only archives trades that fail validation
quiz.exp: Raw archiving is a deliberate SOX control. The raw archive shows exactly what the source sent and never changes.

activities.title: Resolution queue triage
activities.intro: You've just opened the Resolution Queue and see the following situations. For each one, decide on the correct course of action.

steps.situation: You see 47 trades from Broadridge all showing CLIENT_NOT_FOUND for Rep Code 'BRD-9921'. This Rep Code has no alias in the system.
opts: Manually link each of the 47 trades one by one to the closest matching advisor
Escalate to the Data Steward to create the missing entity record and alias, then allow trades to reprocess automatically
Delete all 47 trades and ask Broadridge to resubmit
Mark all 47 as exceptions and move on
steps.exp: Batch errors with the same root cause need fixing at the source. The missing alias is a Data Steward task — once created, all 47 trades reprocess automatically.

steps.situation: A single high-value trade ($4.2M) has been in the queue for 3 days showing PRODUCT_NOT_FOUND for CUSIP 45678XYZ.
opts: Wait — it will resolve itself overnight
Escalate to your manager immediately and investigate the CUSIP — a trade this size should not sit unresolved for 3 days
Delete the trade and ask the source to resubmit
Link it to any product with a similar name
steps.exp: High-value trades ($1M+) unresolved for more than 24 hours require immediate manager escalation. The CUSIP needs to be investigated and mapped to the correct product.

steps.situation: A trade arrives from source code 'LPL_NEW_FEED' that Synfinii doesn't recognise, generating SOURCE_CODE_NOT_FOUND. There are 200 trades with the same error.
opts: Try to manually configure the source yourself
Escalate to the IT Administrator — an unconfigured source feed needs to be set up before these trades can process
Delete all 200 trades
Link each trade manually to an existing source
steps.exp: SOURCE_CODE_NOT_FOUND indicates an unconfigured feed — this is an IT Administrator task and cannot be resolved from the cockpit. Manual resolution of 200 trades would be inefficient and doesn't fix the root cause.

modules.title: The Resolution Queue Interface
modules.desc: Navigate the Resolution Cockpit, prioritize your workload, and complete the full resolution cycle.

lessons.title: The Resolution Cockpit interface
lessons.content:
The Resolution Cockpit (Operations > Resolution) is where operations teams clear financial processing errors. It has two levels:

Resolution Queue — lists all active error groups. Each group represents candidates sharing the same error type and the same identifying values (the grouping key). Each row shows the error code, grouping key, record count, source code, and impact metrics (AUM, sales, redemptions affected). Use the impact metrics to prioritize — resolve high-AUM errors first. The default sort order prioritizes by total sales impact, then AUM, then record count.

The queue also has a 'Resolved Today' tab showing groups resolved in the current session, with a link to the underlying candidate detail for quick verification.

Threshold filtering: when AUM and/or sales thresholds are configured for a source code, low-value client error groups are automatically hidden from the queue. A group is hidden only if it falls below both thresholds. Non-client errors (product, transaction code, currency, etc.) are never filtered.

Resolution Cockpit (per error group) — a split-panel view opened by clicking any queue row:

Left panel — shows the Error Context Card with the specific values that caused the error (highlighted in yellow), a sample record organized by sections (Product, Transaction, Account, Dates, Source), and an error description with context-specific guidance. When partial resolution occurred, the left panel also shows 'Original Codes' (raw codes before any transformation) and 'Resolved Client' (hierarchy levels successfully resolved before the error).

Right panel — contains Smart Suggestions (automated match candidates ranked by confidence score), resolution action forms, a Workflow Toggle for switching between Firm/Rep/Account workflows, and an 'Apply to all records' checkbox (checked by default — always leave this checked to resolve the entire error group at once).

Smart Suggestions use multiple matching strategies: fuzzy text matching, exact alias matching, pattern matching (e.g. Firm/*/Rep wildcards), and Common Client (CCOP) database lookups. High-confidence suggestions are highlighted green. Click 'Best Match' to auto-populate the resolution form.

Resolution audit: when an Audit Configuration is enabled, submitting a resolution displays a dialog prompting for a Reason and free-text Notes. Both are stored on each repaired process record and appear in the Candidate Detail panel — providing a searchable audit trail of every resolution decision.

lessons.title: Filtering and triaging your queue
lessons.content:
The Resolution Queue groups errors by type and identifying values. Use the impact metrics columns to prioritize work:

Priority 1 — PRODUCT_NOT_FOUND and TRANSACTION_CODE_NOT_FOUND. These are fatal errors affecting the most records. Fix reference data first.

Priority 2 — PRODUCT_PRICE_NOT_FOUND and FX_RATE_NOT_FOUND. Date-specific errors — batch by date range for efficient resolution.

Priority 3 — CURRENCY_CODE_NOT_FOUND. Usually few records; quick to fix.

Priority 4 — FIRM_NOT_FOUND. Client hierarchy errors; may partially self-resolve if the CCOP database is configured.

Priority 5 — CLIENT_NOT_FOUND. Large volume in initial runs; complex workflow with three resolution modes (Create Alias, Reassign Codes, Default to House).

Priority 6 — ACCOUNTCLIENT_NOT_FOUND. Requires knowledge of account-client relationships.

Priority 7 — SOURCE_CODE_NOT_FOUND. Administrative error; escalate immediately to IT — no resolution available from the cockpit.

Within each error type, sort by Impact AUM descending to address the highest-value unresolved records first.

lessons.title: Batch errors vs. individual exceptions
lessons.content:
Batch errors look like 50 records from Broadridge all showing PRODUCT_NOT_FOUND for the same CUSIP, or 30 records from LPL all showing CLIENT_NOT_FOUND for the same Rep Code.

For batch errors, the right action is NOT to resolve each record individually. Identify the root cause and fix it once. The records then reprocess automatically.

The 'Apply to all records' checkbox in the Resolution Cockpit is checked by default — this ensures your resolution action applies to the entire error group simultaneously, not just the single sample record shown.

lessons.title: Escalation paths from the queue
lessons.content:
Escalate to Data Steward when: the trade's identifier matches an entity that doesn't exist yet, or the matching entity has data quality issues causing the failure.

Escalate to IT Administrator when: a new source feed is generating SOURCE_CODE_NOT_FOUND errors because it hasn't been configured, or a recurring batch error suggests a rule or mapping needs updating.

Escalate to your manager when: a trade has been in the queue for more than 5 business days, or a high-value trade ($1M+) has been unresolved for more than 24 hours.

lessons.title: Navigation and where things live
lessons.content:
Knowing where to find things in the application saves time. Here are the key pages for Resolution Services work:

Financial menu → Candidates → Resolution Queue — your primary workspace. This is where you start every session. Error groups are listed here sorted by financial impact.

Financial menu → Candidates → Candidates — the raw candidate browser. You can search and filter all financial candidates, see their current status (pending, success, failed, excluded), and inspect individual process records.

Financial menu → Transactions — fact records created from successfully processed candidates. After you resolve an error group and it reprocesses, navigate here to confirm the records appear with correct attribution.

Financial menu → Products → Classes — the product master. When resolving PRODUCT_NOT_FOUND errors, you can open this in a second tab to check whether the product exists before creating a new one.

Clients menu — the entity master for firms, offices, and contacts. Useful when resolving CLIENT_NOT_FOUND and FIRM_NOT_FOUND errors to look up the correct profile before creating an alias.

Process records: each candidate has a process record that tracks its journey through the pipeline. The process record shows the candidate's current status, any error code, the grouping key, and all resolved values (product_class_id, trade_class, resolved_profile_id, etc.). You can access process records directly from a candidate's detail page.

lessons.title: The complete resolution cycle
lessons.content:
Understanding the full cycle — from error to verified fact record — gives you a mental model for your daily work. Here is the complete flow:

1. Processing runs — the pipeline processes candidates. Failed candidates get an error_code and grouping_key assigned. They appear in the Resolution Queue grouped by error type and identifying value.

2. You open the queue — sorted by financial impact. The top row tells you the highest-dollar group to address first. A group showing $12.5M sales impact means $12.5M will not appear in any report until resolved.

3. You open the cockpit — click the row. Left panel shows you what failed and why. Right panel gives you Smart Suggestions and the resolution form.

4. You resolve — create an alias, map a transaction code, enter a price, etc. The 'Apply to all records' checkbox (checked by default) applies your fix to every record in the group simultaneously.

5. Records are marked 'repaired' — the pipeline automatically picks up repaired records in the next processing run, or you can trigger reprocessing immediately.

6. Reprocessing runs — the repaired records go through the pipeline again with the new reference data in place.

7. You verify — the error group disappears from the queue. Navigate to Financial > Transactions to confirm the records appear as fact records with correct product identification, trade classification, and client attribution.

The cycle repeats for each error group. As you work through PRODUCT_NOT_FOUND first, then TRANSACTION_CODE_NOT_FOUND, then client errors, the queue shrinks and the fact tables fill with accurate data.

quiz.q: You open the Resolution Queue and see errors of multiple types. Which do you tackle first?
opts: Sort alphabetically A to Z
PRODUCT_NOT_FOUND and TRANSACTION_CODE_NOT_FOUND — these are fatal reference data errors affecting the most records
Filter to records older than 90 days
Show only SOURCE_CODE_NOT_FOUND
quiz.exp: PRODUCT_NOT_FOUND and TRANSACTION_CODE_NOT_FOUND are the highest priority — they are fatal errors affecting the most records. Fix reference data first.

quiz.q: The queue shows PRODUCT_NOT_FOUND on 15 trades. What does this tell you?
opts: The trades are from an unauthorized source
The product identifier (CUSIP, ticker, or fund code) doesn't match any product in Synfinii's product master
The product has been discontinued
The trades are duplicates
quiz.exp: PRODUCT_NOT_FOUND means none of the product lookup strategies (CUSIP, ticker, fund code alias, fund name alias) found a match.

quiz.q: 50 trades from the same source have the same error. What is the most efficient approach?
opts: Resolve each of the 50 individually
Identify the root cause, fix it once, and allow the records to reprocess automatically
Escalate all 50 to management
Mark all 50 as exceptions and move on
quiz.exp: Batch errors with the same root cause should be fixed at the source. The 'Apply to all records' checkbox ensures your fix resolves the entire group.

quiz.q: You've just resolved a PRODUCT_NOT_FOUND error group and reprocessing has run. How do you confirm it worked?
opts: Trust that it worked — the system is automatic
Check that the error group is gone from the queue, then navigate to Financial > Transactions to confirm the records appear as fact records with correct attribution
Email the Data Steward to verify
Wait until the next morning's report
quiz.exp: Always verify: first confirm the error group is gone from the queue, then check Financial > Transactions to see the records appear as completed fact records with product, classification, and client attribution all populated.

modules.title: Trade/Asset-to-Entity Matching
modules.desc: Manually link unassigned trades to the correct Rep Master Record.

lessons.title: The matching hierarchy
lessons.content:
Synfinii uses a waterfall of match keys:

1. Tax ID — strongest identifier. High-confidence match.
2. CRD number — FINRA's unique identifier. Also high-confidence.
3. Rep Code — medium confidence (same code may appear in multiple systems).
4. Account number — high confidence when clean.
5. Fuzzy matching — name + firm + geography. Low confidence. Always requires human review.

lessons.title: Using Synfinii's lookup tools
lessons.content:
Master record search — search by name, identifier, or firm.

Identifier cross-reference — enter an identifier from the trade and see what entity it maps to across all sources.

Hierarchy view — check that the firm on the trade matches the entity's current firm and that the entity is active.

External references — FINRA BrokerCheck, your internal CRM. Document your research path.

lessons.title: Validating the match before linking
lessons.content:
Before linking a trade:
✓ Identifier confirmation — does the identifier match the entity with at least one corroborating data point?
✓ Activity check — is the entity active on the trade date?
✓ Firm check — does the trade's source firm match the entity's firm on the trade date?
✓ Hierarchy check — is the entity correctly linked to a parent office and national account?

lessons.title: Creating permanent aliases
lessons.content:
When you resolve a CLIENT_NOT_FOUND or FIRM_NOT_FOUND error in the Resolution Cockpit, you are creating a permanent alias — a record in the alias table that links the incoming trade codes (firm, office, rep, qualifier) to an entity profile.

Create Alias — the standard resolution mode. Links the incoming trade codes to an existing contact or firm profile permanently. All future records with the same code combination will resolve to the same entity automatically without human intervention.

Reassign Alias — a one-time override. Overwrites the trade codes in the current batch of staging records with different codes that already have a valid alias. No permanent alias is created. Future records with the original codes will still error. Use this only when the source system sent incorrect codes this time.

Choosing correctly matters: an incorrectly created permanent alias will auto-misattribute every future trade with those codes. If you are not confident the codes are stable and correct, use Reassign for the current batch and investigate before creating the permanent alias.

quiz.q: A trade with Rep Code '7842' has no match. Your lookup returns two similar advisors. How do you confirm which is correct?
opts: Choose the one with the most recent activity
Cross-reference additional identifiers (Tax ID, CRD, firm name) and verify the hierarchy matches
Choose randomly
Reject the trade and ask the source to resubmit
quiz.exp: Always verify using multiple identifiers.

quiz.q: Before promoting a linked trade, what final check should you perform?
opts: Confirm the advisor's birthday is on file
Verify the advisor is active, their firm matches the trade's source, and the hierarchy is valid at the trade date
Check the trade value is above the minimum threshold
Confirm the trade was submitted on a business day
quiz.exp: Hierarchy validity at the time of the trade is critical.

quiz.q: Synfinii offers to save an alias permanently for future transactions. Should you always accept?
opts: Yes — always accept to reduce workload
Only accept if you're confident these trade codes are stable and correct — an incorrect alias will misattribute every future trade with those codes
Never accept — every trade should be reviewed manually
Only accept for trades over $1 million
quiz.exp: Permanent aliases are the standard resolution path — but they must be correct. An alias links specific trade codes to a specific entity permanently. If the codes are wrong or unstable, use Reassign Alias for the current batch and investigate before committing to a permanent alias.

modules.title: Firm/Office Hierarchy Resolution
modules.desc: Assign trades to the correct Firm/Office Hierarchy when data is missing or ambiguous.

lessons.title: Why hierarchy matters for attribution
lessons.content: Every trade needs two things: a match to an entity AND a valid hierarchy link (entity → office → national account). Without the hierarchy link, the trade is attributed to the advisor but doesn't roll up correctly. This silently corrupts roll-up reporting — national account managers see understated AUM.

lessons.title: Handling office-level ambiguity
lessons.content:
When a trade arrives attributed to 'Merrill Lynch' but without an office identifier:

1. Assign it to the Merrill Lynch national account as a placeholder
2. Document this as pending in your resolution notes
3. Monitor for the direct Merrill feed that will provide granular detail
4. When the direct feed arrives, the parent-child account structure handles reconciliation automatically.

lessons.title: Historical attribution and advisor moves
lessons.content:
The rule is simple: attribution reflects where the advisor WAS at the time of the trade, not where they ARE now.

Synfinii tracks advisor moves using effective dating — every hierarchy relationship has a start and end date. A February trade for an advisor who moved in March should be attributed to their February firm.

lessons.title: Repairing broken hierarchy links
lessons.content:
To repair a broken link:
1. Identify the correct parent entity using master record search
2. Verify the parent is active and correctly configured
3. Use the hierarchy management tool to re-establish the parent-child link
4. Set the effective date correctly
5. Check whether any previously attributed trades need to be re-evaluated
6. Document the repair with the reason for the break

quiz.q: A trade came in attributed to 'Merrill Lynch' without an office identifier. What is the correct approach?
opts: Assign to the Merrill Lynch national account as a placeholder until more granular data arrives
Reject the trade — it cannot be processed without an office ID
Assign to a random Merrill Lynch office
Leave it in the queue indefinitely
quiz.exp: Assigning to the national account as a placeholder is correct. When direct feed data arrives, the parent-child relationship handles automatic reconciliation.

quiz.q: An advisor moved from New York to Florida last month. A trade dated before the move is in the queue. Which office receives attribution?
opts: Florida — that's where the advisor is now
New York — attribution reflects where the advisor was at the time of the trade
Both offices share 50% attribution
The trade should be voided
quiz.exp: Attribution is always historical.

quiz.q: A parent-child hierarchy link is broken. What is the impact if you don't fix it?
opts: No impact — trades still process correctly
Roll-up reporting breaks — trades at the office won't aggregate to the national account
The trades will be automatically rejected
Only new trades are affected
quiz.exp: Broken hierarchy links silently corrupt roll-up reporting.

modules.title: Finalizing & Promoting Resolved Data
modules.desc: Promote resolved transactions to the master record and understand downstream impact.

lessons.title: What promotion means
lessons.content:
Promotion moves a resolved trade from the exception queue into Synfinii's master reporting tables — the same tables that feed AUM dashboards, sales performance reports, and SalesPage compensation calculations.

Promotion is not reversible in the normal workflow. Once a trade is in the master tables, correcting it requires a Data Steward to update the entity record and potentially a reprocessing of downstream reports.

lessons.title: The downstream impact of your decisions
lessons.content:
SalesPage — receives promoted trades and uses them to calculate advisor and firm-level AUM, which drives sales compensation.

Sales reporting dashboards — pull from the master tables you're promoting into.

AUM reporting — figures used in investor communications and regulatory filings.

Compliance reports — audit evidence packages include data from the master tables.

Every trade you promote matters.

lessons.title: Escalation after promotion: when things go wrong
lessons.content:
1. Do not try to fix it yourself in the master table.
2. Document the issue immediately.
3. Contact the Data Steward to correct the master entity record.
4. Identify which downstream reports were affected.
5. Escalate to your manager — post-promotion corrections often require coordination and compliance documentation.

lessons.title: Documentation best practices
lessons.content:
A complete resolution note includes:
— What the trade was (source, date, value, error type)
— What you researched (which identifiers, what external sources)
— What you found (the entity matched and why you're confident)
— What action you took
— Any caveats (e.g. 'best available match based on Rep Code — no CRD available to confirm')

Undocumented resolutions are the most common audit finding in Resolution Services workflows.

lessons.title: Verifying your work after reprocessing
lessons.content:
After resolving an error group and triggering reprocessing, always verify the outcome. Don't assume it worked.

Step 1: Check the Resolution Queue — the error group should have disappeared, or the record count should have dropped. If the same group is still there with the same count, reprocessing may not have run yet, or the resolution didn't take effect correctly.

Step 2: Navigate to Financial > Transactions — find the records that were in the error group. They should now appear as transaction fact records with all fields populated: correct product class, trade type and class, gross amount, and client attribution (firm, office, contact).

Step 3: Check the attribution — does the firm and contact shown on the fact record match what you expected when you created the alias? If not, the alias may have been created with incorrect codes.

Step 4: Spot-check the financial values — does the gross amount look reasonable for this type of transaction? An order of magnitude error in amounts sometimes indicates a negation flag was set incorrectly when mapping a transaction code.

If anything looks wrong at steps 2–4, do not try to fix it directly in the fact tables. Document the discrepancy and contact the Data Steward to correct the master record, which will trigger reprocessing of the affected records.

quiz.q: Before promoting a resolved trade, what is the most important final check?
opts: That the trade has a comment attached
That the entity link, product mapping, and transaction type are all confirmed correct
That manager approval has been obtained for every trade
That the trade was resolved within 24 hours
quiz.exp: Promotion pushes data into master tables that feed SalesPage, compensation, and sales reports.

quiz.q: You promoted a trade but realize it was linked to the wrong advisor. What is the correct next step?
opts: Edit the trade directly in the master table
Contact the Data Steward to correct the master record and coordinate re-evaluation of affected downstream reports
Delete the trade and resubmit from scratch
No action needed — minor errors self-correct
quiz.exp: Post-promotion corrections require coordination.

quiz.q: How does your resolution work affect sales compensation for advisors?
opts: It has no effect — compensation is calculated separately
Directly — trades attributed correctly contribute to advisor AUM and flow totals, which drive commission calculations in SalesPage
It only affects dashboards, not compensation
Compensation is calculated manually and doesn't use Synfinii data
quiz.exp: Every trade you resolve correctly contributes to an advisor's attributed AUM and flows.

sr.label: Sales Reporting
sr.desc: Transform mastered data into actionable insights: AUM, flows, territory performance.

modules.title: Dashboard Navigation & Interpretation
modules.desc: Navigate and interpret AUM, flows, and compensation dashboards.

lessons.title: Understanding Synfinii's reporting layer
lessons.content:
All the data that Synfinii's Data Stewards curate and Resolution Services specialists attribute ends up in a reporting layer powered by ThoughtSpot — an AI-driven analytics platform embedded directly in Synfinii.

Pre-built dashboards — curated views for standard questions: total AUM by territory, net flows this quarter, top producers.

Self-service search — a natural language search bar where you type questions like 'Top 10 advisors by AUM in the Midwest last quarter' and ThoughtSpot generates instant visualizations.

lessons.title: Key metrics: AUM and flows
lessons.content:
AUM (Assets Under Management) — the total market value of investments managed on behalf of clients. It's the primary KPI because it directly drives management fees, compensation, and business decisions.

Flows — the movement of money in and out of investment products. Net flows = purchases (inflows) minus redemptions (outflows). Positive net flows mean the business is growing.

Other key metrics: new producers, active accounts, and compensation basis.

lessons.title: Data freshness and the nightly cycle
lessons.content:
Synfinii's reporting data reflects the previous nightly cycle's promotion — typically current as of end-of-business the previous day.

When you present AUM to leadership, state the effective date: 'AUM as of market close yesterday.'

Intraday trades will not appear until the next nightly cycle completes.

lessons.title: Navigating dashboards and applying filters
lessons.content:
Date range filter — selects the time period. Verify which date range is active before presenting.

Territory/Geography filter — scopes to a specific geographic area or sales territory.

Product type filter — filters by investment vehicle (mutual funds, ETFs, separate accounts).

Filters are additive — each one further narrows the data. If a dashboard seems to show less data than expected, check all active filters.

lessons.title: Live Boards vs. traditional dashboards
lessons.content:
ThoughtSpot calls its dashboards 'Live Boards' — and the distinction matters. A traditional dashboard is a static layout of pre-built charts. A Live Board is dynamic: you can interact with it, drill into data, and explore without leaving the screen or building a new report.

Key Live Board capabilities:

Multi-level drill-down — right-click any data point and drill into contributing dimensions.

Underlying data access — hover over a visualization and pull up the raw records behind it.

Multi-tab organization — a single Live Board can have multiple tabs.

Present mode — transforms a Live Board into a presentation-ready view.

Watch list — configure a Live Board as a personal control panel.

lessons.title: AI Highlights: automated insight surfacing
lessons.content:
ThoughtSpot includes an AI Highlights feature that automatically analyzes KPI visualizations on a Live Board and surfaces key findings — without you having to ask.

AI Highlights activates (the button turns blue) when a KPI-type visualization is present on the Live Board. When triggered, the AI scans the data behind that KPI and identifies what is driving the result — specific dealer names, products, CUSIPs, territories, or time periods.

Use AI Highlights at the start of any analysis session to quickly surface what's worth investigating before you start drilling. Think of it as your automated first-pass analyst.

quiz.q: What does AUM stand for and why is it the primary KPI on most dashboards?
opts: Annual Utility Metric — measures system uptime
Assets Under Management — the total value of client investments, which directly drives revenue and compensation
Automated User Module — a system feature
Account Update Method — a data refresh process
quiz.exp: AUM drives management fees, compensation, and business decisions.

quiz.q: A dashboard shows 'Net Flows: -$50M' for a territory this quarter. What does this indicate?
opts: The data feed is broken
More money left the territory's investment products (redemptions) than came in (purchases) — a net outflow
The territory gained $50M in new assets
The compensation calculation is pending
quiz.exp: Net flows = purchases minus redemptions. A negative number means clients withdrew more than they invested.

quiz.q: You need to see AUM data for only the Midwest territory in Q2. Which two filters apply?
opts: Product type + source system
Territory (Midwest) + date range (Q2)
Advisor name + fund manager
Channel type + AUM threshold
quiz.exp: Territory and date range are the two most common filters for geographic and time-based analysis.

quiz.q: A colleague says 'the Live Board is just a fancier dashboard.' What key capability proves them wrong?
opts: Live Boards use better color palettes
Live Boards support unlimited multi-level drill-down, underlying data access, and real-time interaction without building new reports
Live Boards refresh every 5 minutes automatically
Live Boards can only be viewed in present mode
quiz.exp: The core differentiator is interactivity: drill-down without limits, access to underlying records, and real-time exploration.

quiz.q: AI Highlights identifies 'the West territory is the primary driver of this month's purchase spike.' What should you do next?
opts: Accept the insight and close the Live Board
Use it as a starting point — drill into the West territory to understand which offices, advisors, or products are responsible
Disable AI Highlights to avoid bias in your analysis
Forward the insight directly to leadership without further investigation
quiz.exp: AI Highlights surfaces where to look, not the full story.

activities.title: ThoughtSpot concepts — fill in the blank
activities.intro: Complete each statement using the correct ThoughtSpot or Synfinii reporting term. Type your answer in the blank — spelling counts, so check your work before submitting.

blanks.prompt: ThoughtSpot's dynamic dashboards are called ___ because you can drill into data, access underlying records, and interact without leaving the screen.
blanks.exp: Live Boards are ThoughtSpot's interactive dashboards — distinct from traditional static dashboards.

blanks.prompt: Net flows are calculated as purchases minus ___.
blanks.exp: Net flows = purchases (inflows) minus redemptions (outflows). A negative net flow means more money left than came in.

blanks.prompt: ThoughtSpot's AI search interface, which lets you type a business question in plain English and generates analysis automatically, is called ___.
blanks.exp: Spotter is ThoughtSpot's natural language AI search — type a question and it builds the analysis for you.

blanks.prompt: Synfinii's reporting data updates via a nightly ___, which means AUM figures reflect end-of-previous-day data.
blanks.exp: The nightly cycle processes all transactions and updates the master reporting tables.

blanks.prompt: The ThoughtSpot feature that automatically analyses KPI visualizations and surfaces key drivers without you asking is called AI ___.
blanks.exp: AI Highlights scans the data behind a KPI and tells you what's driving the result — your automated first-pass analyst.

modules.title: Filtering, Grouping & Segmentation
modules.desc: Segment data by product line, distribution channel, and territory.

lessons.title: Why segmentation matters
lessons.content: Raw data tells you what happened. Segmented data tells you where, for whom, and why. AUM by territory shows which regions are growing vs. contracting. AUM by product type shows which products are gaining vs. losing share. AUM by distribution channel shows your strongest growth driver.

lessons.title: Grouping and aggregation functions
lessons.content:
GROUP BY — tells the system which dimension to segment by.

Aggregation function:
— SUM: total AUM or flows within each group (most common)
— COUNT: number of accounts or advisors within each group
— AVERAGE: mean AUM per advisor
— MAX/MIN: highest or lowest value

Most sales reporting questions combine both: 'Show me total AUM (SUM) by territory (GROUP BY territory).'

lessons.title: Distribution channel segmentation
lessons.content:
Channels include:
— Wirehouse: large national broker-dealers (Merrill Lynch, Morgan Stanley, UBS, Wells Fargo)
— Independent broker-dealer: smaller BD networks (LPL, Raymond James)
— RIA: fee-based advisory firms
— Bank channel
— Institutional

Channel or Firm Type is the standard segmentation field for distribution analysis.

lessons.title: Time-series analysis
lessons.content:
Comparing data across time periods lets you:
— Compare period-over-period: current quarter vs. prior quarter
— Track trends: monthly AUM for the last 12 months
— Identify seasonality: do flows spike in Q4?
— Measure growth rate: year-over-year growth in a specific channel

Presenting a trend is almost always more compelling and informative than a single point-in-time number.

quiz.q: You want to compare ETF vs. mutual fund performance side by side. Which approach is correct?
opts: Create two separate reports manually
Apply a GROUP BY product type filter to see aggregated totals for each category in one view
Export all data to Excel and sort manually
Run the report twice with different date ranges
quiz.exp: GROUP BY product type aggregates all records within each category.

quiz.q: A sales manager asks for AUM broken down by distribution channel. What field do you use?
opts: Rep Code
Channel or Firm Type — which maps each account to its distribution category
Product CUSIP
Trade date
quiz.exp: Channel or Firm Type is the standard segmentation field for distribution analysis.

quiz.q: After applying multiple filters, your report shows fewer records than expected. What should you check first?
opts: Assume the data is correct
Review each active filter — one may be too restrictive or incorrectly set
Rebuild the report from scratch
Contact IT to report a bug
quiz.exp: Multiple active filters compound their effects. A filter left active from a previous session is a common culprit.

modules.title: Advanced Report Builder Mastery
modules.desc: Design, save, and share custom reports using the drag-and-drop Report Builder.

lessons.title: The report builder interface
lessons.content:
Key components:

Data fields panel — all available fields from the Synfinii data model. Drag fields into your report.

Canvas area — build your report here. Drop fields to add columns, define groupings, and set aggregations.

Filter panel — add filters to scope the data.

Visualization options — toggle between table view, bar chart, line graph, heat map, or pie chart.

Calculated fields — create derived metrics (e.g. 'Net flows = purchases - redemptions').

lessons.title: Building a monthly flow report
lessons.content:
Step 1: Select fields — Territory, Month (from transaction date), Purchases (SUM), Redemptions (SUM).
Step 2: Add a calculated field — 'Net Flows = Purchases - Redemptions.'
Step 3: Set grouping — GROUP BY Territory, then by Month.
Step 4: Apply date filter — last 12 months.
Step 5: Choose visualization — line chart for trends, table for sharing exact figures.
Step 6: Validate — cross-check the largest territory's total against a known figure.
Step 7: Save with a descriptive name and schedule for distribution.

lessons.title: Report performance optimization
lessons.content:
Always apply a date range filter — querying 5 years of history when you need 1 quarter dramatically increases load time.

Filter before grouping — narrow the dataset first, then apply groupings and aggregations.

Avoid unnecessary columns — every additional field adds to query complexity.

Test with a small date range first — validate the logic is correct before committing to a longer runtime.

lessons.title: Sharing, permissions, and scheduling
lessons.content:
View-only link — recipients can see data but cannot modify the report definition.

Edit access — recipients can modify the report. Use only for trusted colleagues co-developing the report.

Scheduled distribution — automated email delivery at a set frequency. Recipients receive a snapshot without needing a Synfinii login.

Before sharing any report containing compensation data, confirm the recipient has appropriate authorization.

lessons.title: ThoughtSpot Spotter: AI-powered natural language search
lessons.content:
ThoughtSpot's AI search interface is called Spotter. Rather than dragging and dropping fields, you type a business question in plain English and Spotter builds the analysis for you.

Example question: 'Exclusively in the RIA channel, what are the top 10 redeeming offices I should focus on for retention efforts?'

Spotter identifies the correct fields automatically, suggests a visualization, generates written insights, and lets you edit the result immediately.

Column sets — define custom groupings. For example, create a new territory by selecting specific states and label it 'N Territory Expansion 1.'

lessons.title: Formulas, parameters, and query sets in ThoughtSpot
lessons.content:
1. Formulas — create calculated metrics within an answer. ThoughtSpot supports complex logical formulas similar to Excel.

2. Parameters — user-defined inputs that can modify formula behavior.

3. Column sets — define custom groupings of existing dimension values.

4. Query sets — pre-defined filters or conditions that can be reused across analyses.

Important scope rule: Formulas and column sets you create in a Spotter answer are scoped to that answer only. To reuse them, clone the answer or ask an Administrator to add the formula to the shared data model.

quiz.q: You're building a monthly AUM report by territory. Which aggregation applies to the AUM field?
opts: COUNT — to count accounts
SUM — to total all AUM within each territory group
AVERAGE — to find mean AUM per advisor
MIN — to find the smallest account
quiz.exp: SUM aggregates total AUM within each territory group.

quiz.q: Your report takes 3 minutes to load. What is the most likely cause and solution?
opts: The report is too accurate and needs simplification
The report is querying too much historical data without filters — apply a date range to limit the dataset
The server is broken — contact IT
Reports always take this long
quiz.exp: Large, unfiltered datasets cause slow reports. Applying a date range dramatically reduces the data volume.

quiz.q: A colleague needs your custom report every Monday. What is the correct way to share it?
opts: Email them a PDF each Monday manually
Use the scheduling feature to auto-distribute at 7am every Monday with view-only access
Share your login credentials
Print and leave on their desk
quiz.exp: Scheduled distribution automates delivery with defined access levels.

quiz.q: When should you use Spotter's natural language search instead of the Report Builder?
opts: Always — natural language is faster for everything
For exploratory analysis where you're not sure exactly what you're looking for yet
Only when the Report Builder is unavailable
Spotter is only for administrators
quiz.exp: Spotter excels at exploratory analysis. The Report Builder is better for structured, repeatable reports.

quiz.q: You create a formula in a Spotter answer. A colleague wants to use it in their own answer. What is the correct approach?
opts: The formula is automatically available to everyone
Clone your answer and share it, or ask an Administrator to add it to the shared data model
Re-type the formula in each new answer manually
Export the formula as a CSV and import it elsewhere
quiz.exp: Formulas created in a Spotter answer are scoped to that answer. Cloning shares them; adding to the data model makes them globally available.

modules.title: Data Comprehension & Validation
modules.desc: Validate report accuracy and understand data lineage.

lessons.title: Why validation is your responsibility
lessons.content: You are the last checkpoint before data reaches stakeholders. A report presented to leadership with incorrect AUM figures can lead to wrong business decisions, compensation disputes, and regulatory issues. Validation isn't optional box-checking — it's the professional standard.

lessons.title: Cross-referencing and spot-checking
lessons.content:
Spot-check against known values — if a major advisor had ~$500M last quarter and this quarter shows $50M, investigate before distributing.

Period-over-period comparison — changes of more than 10-15% in a single quarter need a documented explanation.

Sum-of-parts check — the sum of all territories should equal the total AUM figure.

Line-item drill-down — for key figures, drill into underlying records to confirm the data is correct.

lessons.title: Understanding data lineage for validation
lessons.content: When a number looks wrong, data lineage helps you trace it to the source. Click on an aggregate figure to drill into underlying records. From there, see which source system the data came from, when it was promoted, and whether any records have unresolved exceptions in the Resolution Queue.

lessons.title: Communicating data quality issues to stakeholders
lessons.content:
Best practice: present the report with a clear caveat. 'AUM figures as of [date] — note that approximately $30M in ETF trades are currently pending resolution and are not yet reflected. Updated figures will be available by [date].'

What not to do: present the report without disclosing the known gap. Data quality transparency builds trust over time.

quiz.q: Your Q3 AUM report shows $500M for a territory but the manager says it should be $550M. What is your investigation process?
opts: Accept the manager's number and adjust the report
Drill into underlying transactions and check the Resolution Queue for unresolved trades affecting the total
Tell the manager the system is always correct
Delete the report and start over
quiz.exp: The $50M gap is likely explained by trades still in the Resolution Queue or a hierarchy break.

quiz.q: What is 'data freshness' and why does it matter when presenting to leadership?
opts: Whether the report design looks modern
How recently the underlying data was updated — leaders need to know if they're looking at yesterday's data or last week's
The number of decimal places displayed
Whether the report has been approved by compliance
quiz.exp: Synfinii updates via nightly cycle — AUM reflects end-of-previous-day data.

quiz.q: Leadership asks 'Where does this AUM number come from?' How do you answer?
opts: The number is calculated by IT and its source is unknown
Use the data lineage view to trace the figure back through the attribution pipeline to its original source feeds
Refer them to the original data provider
The number is proprietary and cannot be traced
quiz.exp: Data lineage provides a traceable path from any reported number back through the processing pipeline.

modules.title: Report Scheduling & Distribution
modules.desc: Automate report delivery and manage access permissions.
