lessons.title: Designing a distribution schedule
lessons.content:
Questions to answer before scheduling:
— What is the right frequency? Daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly?
— When should it deliver? First thing Monday morning or the day after period close?
— What format? PDF for static snapshots or live links for recipients who need to drill in?
— What scope? Territory-scoped reports require separate scheduled instances per recipient.

lessons.title: ThoughtSpot scheduling and threshold alerts
lessons.content:
Scheduled deliveries: From any Live Board, open the 'More' menu and select 'Create schedule.' You can deliver the full Live Board or select specific visualizations. A single Live Board can have multiple schedules delivering different subsets to different distribution lists on different cadences.

Threshold alerts: Alerts are separate from schedules. An alert fires whenever a condition is met. You set a KPI metric, define a trigger condition, and specify who should be notified.

Key distinction: Schedules = regular cadence delivery. Alerts = event-driven notification when a threshold is crossed.

lessons.title: Access control and data governance
lessons.content:
Compensation reports — restrict to the individuals whose compensation is shown, their direct manager, and authorized HR/finance personnel.

Territory reports — each territory manager should receive only their own territory's data.

Before setting up a new distribution list, confirm each recipient's authorization level with your manager or data governance team.

lessons.title: Monitoring and maintaining scheduled reports
lessons.content:
Common failure modes:

Recipient email changes — when someone changes roles or leaves, their distribution address may become invalid.

Report definition becomes stale — if the underlying data model changes, a report built on the old structure may silently produce incorrect output.

Permission revocation — if a recipient's access is revoked but they're still on a scheduled distribution, they continue receiving data they're no longer authorized to see.

quiz.q: You need to distribute a compensation report to 5 regional managers, each seeing only their own territory. How do you configure this?
opts: Send one combined report to all 5
Use territory-scoped views or separate scheduled reports per manager, each filtered to their specific territory
Give all 5 full access for transparency
Manually edit the report 5 times before sending
quiz.exp: Territory scoping ensures each recipient sees only relevant data.

quiz.q: A scheduled report stops delivering every Monday. What is your first troubleshooting step?
opts: Rebuild the report from scratch
Check the scheduling configuration — verify recipient addresses, permissions, and whether the underlying query is still valid
Assume the server is down and wait for IT
Cancel the schedule and switch to manual delivery
quiz.exp: Most scheduling failures are configuration issues.

quiz.q: What is 'least privilege' and how does it apply to report distribution?
opts: Give users the most data possible
Give users only the data access they need to perform their specific job — nothing more
Restrict all data access to senior leadership only
Apply only to financial data
quiz.exp: Least privilege means users get exactly the access their role requires.

quiz.q: A head of product wants to be alerted when any fund's AUM drops by more than 10% in a week, but doesn't log into ThoughtSpot regularly. What is the right solution?
opts: Email them the full Live Board every day
Set a threshold alert on the AUM KPI — it fires only when the condition is met and can be delivered to any email address
Ask them to log in and check manually
Create a separate scheduled weekly report for each fund they manage
quiz.exp: Threshold alerts are event-driven and deliver to email addresses — no platform login required.

ad.label: Administrator
ad.desc: Configure, secure, and maintain Synfinii. Own system stability, access control, and compliance.

modules.title: Platform Orientation & Architecture
modules.desc: Understand Synfinii's architecture, environments, and your role as a SOX control point.

lessons.title: What is Synfinii and why does it matter to an Admin?
lessons.content:
Synfinii is a data management, reporting, and analytics platform for asset managers deployed on Amazon Web Services (AWS). It's a microservice-based system — meaning the platform is made up of multiple independent services (entity management, financial processing, coverage, reporting), each backed by a PostgreSQL database.

For you as an Administrator, the microservice architecture means failures are isolated — a problem in one service doesn't take down the whole platform — and deployments can be incremental.

lessons.title: The three environments
lessons.content:
Development (Dev) — where new features are built and unit-tested. Primarily used by the Synfinii engineering team.

UAT (User Acceptance Testing) — the staging environment. This is where you test ALL configuration changes before they go live.

Production (Prod) — the live environment. Real client data. Real transactions.

The mandatory promotion path is Dev → UAT → Production. This is a SOX Change Management control. There are no exceptions.

lessons.title: Admin vs. Data Steward: Segregation of Duties
lessons.content:
Data Steward — owns the DATA. Creates records, resolves exceptions, corrects data quality issues.

Administrator — owns the SYSTEM. Configures the platform, manages who has access, deploys rules, monitors infrastructure.

This separation is a SOX Segregation of Duties (SoD) requirement. You set up the Data Steward's account and assign their role. You do not then use that role yourself to modify data records.

lessons.title: Your role as a SOX control point
lessons.content:
Your three ITGC areas:

1. Change Management — all system changes documented, tested in UAT, and approved before Production deployment.

2. Access Control — users have only the access their role requires. You provision, you review, you revoke.

3. System Availability — the system meets agreed uptime targets. You monitor, respond to incidents, and maintain tested backup and recovery procedures.

Every action you take in Synfinii is permanently logged. These logs are the primary evidence during SOX audits.

quiz.q: Synfinii's microservice architecture means what operationally for an Administrator?
opts: All functions are handled by one monolithic application
Each platform function is an independent service — failures are isolated and updates can be deployed incrementally
Microservices require more manual configuration
Administrators cannot access individual services directly
quiz.exp: Microservice architecture means failures are isolated. For Admins, incident response is more targeted and deployments can be incremental.

quiz.q: What is the mandatory promotion path for all configuration changes?
opts: Development → Production directly
Development → UAT → Production
UAT → Development → Production
Production → UAT (post-deployment validation)
quiz.exp: Dev → UAT → Production is non-negotiable. Skipping UAT is a SOX Change Management violation.

quiz.q: As a Synfinii Administrator, what does being a 'formal SOX control point' mean?
opts: You are required to attend SOX training annually
Your actions — user provisioning, rule deployments, config changes — are IT General Controls that auditors review as evidence of compliance
SOX only applies to the finance team
You are responsible for filing SOX reports with the SEC
quiz.exp: IT General Controls (ITGCs) are a formal SOX component.

modules.title: User Security & Access Control (RBAC)
modules.desc: Provision users, assign roles, configure SSO/MFA, and conduct access reviews.

lessons.title: Role-Based Access Control fundamentals
lessons.content:
Synfinii uses RBAC — instead of configuring individual permissions for each person, you assign roles (predefined permission sets).

Standard roles: Data Steward, Resolution Services, Sales Reporting, Administrator.

The principle of least privilege applies: assign the minimum role necessary. Don't assign Admin rights to someone who only needs to run reports.

lessons.title: Provisioning a new user
lessons.content:
1. Receive a formal authorized provisioning request — never provision based on a verbal request from the user themselves.
2. Create the account with the user's work email.
3. Assign the appropriate role based on job function, not what they've requested.
4. Scope the access appropriately.
5. Document the provisioning action.
6. Notify the user and their manager.

lessons.title: SSO and MFA configuration
lessons.content:
SSO (Single Sign-On) — connects Synfinii to your firm's identity provider (Azure AD or Okta). Users log in with corporate credentials. Deactivating someone in your identity provider automatically revokes their Synfinii access.

MFA (Multi-Factor Authentication) — requires a second factor beyond the password. Required for all Administrator accounts and any role with access to compensation or financial data.

lessons.title: Periodic access reviews
lessons.content:
Minimum frequency: quarterly for SOX compliance.

The review process:
1. Export the current user list with roles and last login date
2. Share with each user's manager for sign-off
3. Document manager confirmations
4. Revoke access for anyone no longer requiring it
5. Investigate accounts unused for 90+ days
6. Retain documentation as audit evidence

quiz.q: A new Data Steward joins the team. What is the correct provisioning sequence?
opts: Give them Admin access temporarily
Receive an authorized request, create the account, assign only the Data Steward role with appropriate scope, document the action
Let them use a colleague's login
Give them all available roles immediately
quiz.exp: Least privilege from day one: authorized request first, then create account, assign only the required role.

quiz.q: What is the key difference between authentication (SSO/MFA) and authorization (RBAC)?
opts: They are the same thing
Authentication confirms who you are; authorization controls what you can do once logged in
RBAC is only for external users; SSO is only for internal users
Authentication is optional; authorization is mandatory
quiz.exp: SSO/MFA authenticates identity. RBAC authorizes access. Both layers are required.

quiz.q: A quarterly access review reveals a user has both Data Steward and Resolution Services roles. Why is this a compliance concern?
opts: It is not a concern — more roles means more productivity
This violates Segregation of Duties — a user who creates/modifies records shouldn't also resolve financial exceptions for those same records
It's only a concern if they also have Admin access
Access reviews are optional
quiz.exp: SoD requires different stages of a financial process to be controlled by different people.

modules.title: Integration Setup & Feed Management
modules.desc: Configure data feeds, monitor ingestion health, and manage the processing pipeline.

lessons.title: The three components of a feed setup
lessons.content:
Provider — defines WHERE the data comes from: FTP site, credentials, file path, file name pattern.

Source — defines HOW to interpret the data: file format, column mapping, which Synfinii schema applies.

Job — defines WHEN to process and WHAT to do: schedule, dependencies, and output routing.

Navigation: Import > Import Settings > Source Codes is where you configure providers. Each source code record holds both the provider connection details and the processing configuration in one place.

lessons.title: Configuring a source code — fields in detail
lessons.content:
When creating or editing a source code, the key fields are:

Source Code — the short alphanumeric identifier (e.g. FIDELITY, BROADRIDGE_LQE1). Used on every candidate record for lineage and filtering. Choose something stable — changing it later requires updating all downstream references.

Ingestion Protocol — how files are collected:
— LOCAL: files from a folder path on the server (dev/testing)
— SFTP: files pulled from a remote SFTP server using credentials
— S3: files pulled from an AWS S3 bucket
— NONE: no automated ingestion; files uploaded manually

Ingestion Location — the full path or URL where files are found (e.g. /data/feeds/fidelity or s3://bucket/feeds/fidelity).

Match Pattern — controls which files in the location are picked up. Supports glob patterns (e.g. *.csv matches all CSV files) and regex patterns (e.g. ^transactions_\d{8}\.csv$ matches only date-stamped files). Be as specific as possible to avoid picking up partial or temporary files.

Archive Policy — what happens to the source file after successful ingestion:
— DELETE: source file is removed after ingestion (standard for production)
— NONE: source file is left in place (useful during testing or when the source system owns the file)

executing_at — controls how deep into the client hierarchy Stage 9 resolves (NULL, FIRM, or OFFICE). Set this correctly for each source — a source that only sends firm-level identifiers should use FIRM, otherwise the pipeline generates unnecessary CLIENT_NOT_FOUND errors.

lessons.title: Triggering ingestion and reading results
lessons.content:
Once a source code is configured with a protocol other than NONE, a play button appears in the Actions column of the Source Codes table (Import > Import Settings > Source Codes).

To trigger ingestion manually:
1. Find the source code row
2. Click the play button (triangular arrow icon) in the far-right Actions column
3. The button shows a spinning indicator while the system scans the location
4. An alert appears at the top of the page when complete — e.g. 'Ingested 3 file(s) from FIDELITY_DEMO'

The system:
— Scans the ingestion location for files matching the pattern
— Copies each matched file into the internal file repository
— Applies the archive policy to the originals (deletes or leaves them)
— Creates a file record and ingestion job record for each file

If the play button is disabled (greyed out), the protocol is set to NONE or not configured. Hover over the button to see the tooltip explaining why.

In production, ingestion is typically triggered automatically by a pipeline schedule rather than manually.

lessons.title: Monitoring ingestion with the Ingestion Jobs page
lessons.content:
After triggering ingestion — manually or via pipeline — navigate to Import > Import Settings > Ingestion Jobs to review what happened.

The Ingestion Jobs list shows:
— Source Code Name
— Status: COMPLETED (green), PROCESSING (yellow), FAILED (red), PARTIAL_SUCCESS (blue)
— Started At and Finished At timestamps
— Duration
— Files count — how many files were acquired

Click any job row to open the detail dialog, which has three sections:

Summary box — job ID, source code, timestamps, duration, files acquired count.

Execution Logs — a terminal-style log of what happened during the job. When something goes wrong, this is your first diagnostic stop. Look for file-specific errors, path resolution failures, or permission issues.

Ingested Files table — lists every file acquired in this job with its stored path, file size, and upload timestamp. The stored path follows the pattern: sources/{SOURCE_CODE}/{YEAR}/{MONTH}/{DAY}/{filename}. This structured path enables browsing by source and date, and provides complete traceability — any file can be traced back to its source code and acquisition batch.

Volume-based alerts fire when a feed delivers an unusually low or zero number of records. Configure these on the pipeline to catch silent failures — a source that simply stops sending files will not generate an error on its own.

lessons.title: Pre-built source integrations
lessons.content:
Synfinii's library of pre-built source configurations includes: Broadridge, Fidelity BD, InvestNet, Schwab, SalesConnect, Salesforce, LPL, Merrill Lynch, transfer agents and DSAs.

To activate a pre-built source, configure the Provider and Job, then link them to the existing Source configuration. The data mapping is already done.

lessons.title: Event-based processing and job dependencies
lessons.content:
Processing is event-based: files begin processing as soon as they arrive. Job dependencies control orchestration.

A Job configured with dependencies will receive File A — begin staging but not process — then receive File B — now process the complete dataset.

If only File A arrives and File B doesn't, the job holds File A until either File B arrives or the dependency timeout triggers (typically 24 hours).

lessons.title: ThoughtSpot data workspace and org management
lessons.content:
ThoughtSpot uses a multi-org model. There is a primary org where Administrators build and test data models. Each client has their own individual org — completely isolated.

Data workspace workflow:
1. Set up a connection to the data source
2. Select which tables are needed
3. Configure the joins — specify which IDs link each table, the join type, and cardinality
4. Select and configure columns — rename fields, add descriptions, create calculated columns
5. Tag tables for organization

Once built, click 'Search on this model' to validate results.

lessons.title: TML: ThoughtSpot Modeling Language
lessons.content:
TML (ThoughtSpot Modeling Language) is the code representation of any ThoughtSpot object.

Why TML matters:
1. Cross-org deployment — export a model from the primary org and import it into each client org.
2. Version control — TML files can be stored in Git for change history and rollback.
3. Consistency enforcement — all clients receive the same standard model.

Prerequisite: the target org must have the same underlying tables with the same names. Client-created formulas are answer-scoped. Admins can copy the formula logic into the data model via TML to make it globally available.

quiz.q: A new Broadridge ETF feed needs to be configured. What are the three components you must set up?
opts: Database schema, report template, user permissions
Provider (FTP location/credentials), Source (how to process the file), and Job (scheduling and dependencies)
API key, webhook URL, and data model
Encryption key, storage bucket, and backup schedule
quiz.exp: Provider = where; Source = how to interpret; Job = when and how to process.

quiz.q: The nightly cycle failed and three feeds didn't process. What is your first action?
opts: Rerun all feeds immediately
Check system logs to identify which pipeline step failed and determine root cause before escalating
Wait until morning to see if it self-corrects
Delete and re-create all three feed configurations
quiz.exp: Incident response starts with log analysis to understand what failed and why.

quiz.q: A source system sends data via email rather than FTP. When is an import template the right approach?
opts: Never — Synfinii only supports FTP
When the source is non-standard or an infrequent load that doesn't justify building a full automated job
Always — import templates are preferred
Only for files under 1MB
quiz.exp: Import templates are the right tool for irregular, manual, or non-standard sources.

quiz.q: You configure a source code with Match Pattern '*.csv' but the provider also drops '.csv.tmp' partial files during upload. What should you do?
opts: Accept all files — the pipeline will filter out invalid ones
Use a more specific regex pattern (e.g. ^[A-Z]+_\d{8}\.csv$) to match only complete, correctly named files
Set Archive Policy to NONE so partial files are not deleted
Disable the match pattern entirely
quiz.exp: A specific regex pattern prevents partial or temporary files from being picked up mid-upload. Always be as specific as possible with match patterns in production.

quiz.q: After triggering ingestion, where do you go to see the execution logs and confirm which files were acquired?
opts: Control Tower dashboard
Import > Import Settings > Ingestion Jobs — click the job row to open the detail dialog with logs and file list
Financial > Candidates
Admin > Audit Log
quiz.exp: The Ingestion Jobs page (Import > Import Settings > Ingestion Jobs) shows every job with status, duration, and file count. The detail dialog shows execution logs and the full list of acquired files with their stored paths.

quiz.q: You've built and validated a new data model in the primary ThoughtSpot org. How do you deploy it to a client org?
opts: Rebuild it manually in each client org
Export the TML from the primary org and import it into the client org — provided the underlying tables and names match
Send the client a PDF of the model configuration
It deploys automatically when you save the model
quiz.exp: TML export/import is the deployment mechanism. The target org must have matching table names and structure.

quiz.q: A client creates a useful formula in their Spotter answer and wants it available across all their reports. What can you do as an Administrator?
opts: Nothing — client formulas are permanently locked to the answer they were created in
Copy the formula logic into the client's data model via TML, making it globally available within their org
Delete their answer and rebuild it with the formula at the model level
The client must manually re-enter the formula in every answer they create
quiz.exp: Admins have visibility into client-created formulas. Adding the formula to the data model via TML promotes it from answer-scoped to globally available.

modules.title: Business Rules Engine Management
modules.desc: Deploy, monitor, and roll back mastering and matching rules.

lessons.title: The 10-stage financial processing pipeline
lessons.content:
Every trade passes through a fixed 10-stage pipeline, plus a pre-processing Stage 0:

Stage 0 — Pre-Initialization Rules (PRE_INIT): runs domain rules before any pipeline columns are added. Rules here can set override_exclude_flag=True on a candidate, which causes Stage 1 to mark it with candidate_status='excluded' and error_code='EXCLUDED'. Excluded candidates produce no fact records and no error queue entry — they are silently dropped. Typical uses: filter out IWS channel duplicates from a multi-channel feed, suppress known bad data before it enters the pipeline.

Stage 1 — Initialization: prepares the processing batch, adds output columns (error_code, grouping_key, candidate_status, resolved_*).
Stage 2 — Source Code Validation: verifies the source code is configured. Can error: SOURCE_CODE_NOT_FOUND.
Stage 3 — Product Identification: resolves CUSIP, ticker, or fund code to a product class (trade and account candidates). Can error: PRODUCT_NOT_FOUND.
Stage 4 — Market Product Identification: for market flow/position candidates. Can error: MARKET_PRODUCT_NOT_FOUND.
Stage 5 — Transaction Classification: maps transaction code to trade type and class. Can error: TRANSACTION_CODE_NOT_FOUND.
Stage 6 — Currency Resolution: resolves currency; defaults to USD in single-currency environments.
Stage 7 — Valuation: calculates gross amount using product prices and FX rates. Can error: PRODUCT_PRICE_NOT_FOUND, CURRENCY_VALUE_NOT_FOUND (also called FX_RATE_NOT_FOUND).
Stage 8 — Alias Remapping: normalizes firm, office, and qualifier codes before client attribution. Original values are preserved in *_orig columns.
Stage 9 — Client Attribution: resolves the Firm → Office → Contact hierarchy using the alias table, with optional CCOP fallback. Can error: CLIENT_NOT_FOUND, FIRM_NOT_FOUND, ACCOUNT_NOT_FOUND, ACCOUNTCLIENT_NOT_FOUND.
Stage 10 — Business Rules (FINAL_RULES): applies custom rules from the active Release. Runs after all attribution is complete — this is the most common hook point for custom logic.

lessons.title: Types of injectable rules
lessons.content:
Business rules inject custom logic at hook points in the pipeline without code changes. Each rule has three components:

Binding — where and when the rule executes. Hook points include: FINANCIAL_STAGE_0_PRE_INIT (before processing — to exclude candidates), FINANCIAL_STAGE_10_FINAL_RULES (after all stages — the most common), PRE_CREATE / POST_CREATE (entity lifecycle events), and territory-specific hooks.

Conditions — which records the rule fires on (AND logic). Types: Audience Selection (JSON Logic expressions e.g. 'gross_amount > 1,000,000'), Pattern Selection (regex matching against a field), Record Selection (batch lookup against another service table), Watchlist Membership.

Actions — what happens to matching records. Types:
— Set Field: update a field value (constant, template, field copy, regex extract/replace)
— Create Work Item: route for human review with configurable priority (LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH, CRITICAL)
— Watchlist Action: add/remove from monitoring watchlists
— Service Action: create or update records in a related table
— Exclude Candidate: permanently excludes matching candidates (PRE_INIT only)

Workspace/Release lifecycle: Rules are authored in a Workspace — a temporary, isolated environment for development and testing. When ready, the Workspace is activated and produces a Release. Two activation strategies exist:
— REPLACE: the workspace rules replace all rules in the matching Release entirely
— AUGMENT: the workspace rules are merged into the existing Release (new rules added, existing rules updated by ID)

Releases are versioned using Type 2 SCD — each activation clones the Release into a new version, preserving the full history. The current version is always where effective_end_date IS NULL. Old versions are never deleted.

Two-level priority system: multiple Releases can be active simultaneously. Release priority (higher = executes first) controls the order between Releases. Within each Release, each ReleaseRule has its own priority controlling the order within that Release. This lets a 'Hotfixes' Release (priority 100) layer on top of 'Base Rules' (priority 0) without replacing it.

lessons.title: The rule deployment process
lessons.content:
1. Receive the escalated rule change request from the Data Steward.
2. Draft the new or modified rule in Development.
3. Test in UAT — verify it produces expected outcomes and no unexpected side effects.
4. Get sign-off from the Data Steward who escalated the request.
5. Document the change.
6. Deploy to Production following your Change & Release Management runbook.
7. Monitor post-deployment for the first 48 hours.

lessons.title: Rollback and version control
lessons.content:
All rules are versioned — each change creates a new version while preserving history.

When to roll back: a deployed rule is causing more records to fail, or a Data Steward reports unexpected exceptions that started immediately after deployment.

Rollback procedure:
1. Confirm the issue is caused by the new rule
2. Revert to the previous version in Production
3. Document the rollback with reason and timestamp
4. Notify the Data Steward
5. Investigate in Dev before attempting a revised deployment

quiz.q: A Data Steward escalated a rule change request. What is the correct process?
opts: Implement in Production immediately
Test in Dev, validate in UAT with the Data Steward, document the change, deploy to Production with approval
Reject the request — rules can't be changed once configured
Make the change in UAT only and mark it resolved
quiz.exp: All rule changes follow Dev → UAT → Production.

quiz.q: A newly deployed rule is causing unexpected data quality issues in Production. What is the fastest safe resolution?
opts: Delete the rule and all affected records
Roll back to the previous version immediately, then investigate in Dev/UAT before redeploying
Leave the rule running and manually fix each affected record
Disable all rules until the issue is resolved
quiz.exp: Rollback to the last known good version stops the issue immediately.

quiz.q: You need to deploy a small change that adds two new rules without removing the existing 15 rules in the Base Rules release. Which workspace strategy do you choose?
opts: REPLACE — to replace all 15 rules with the new ones
AUGMENT — to merge the two new rules into the existing release, leaving the other 15 unchanged
Create a new release with a different name
Edit the rules directly in Production
quiz.exp: AUGMENT merges workspace rules into the existing release. REPLACE would overwrite all 15 existing rules with only the 2 new ones — use REPLACE only when you want to completely redefine the rule set.

quiz.q: A rule uses the FINANCIAL_STAGE_0_PRE_INIT hook point. What is its purpose?
opts: Maintenance windows where processing is paused
To exclude matching candidates from all further processing before any pipeline stages run — excluded candidates produce no fact records and no error queue entry
Error checkpoints that auto-reject bad records
Logging events only — no action can be taken at this stage
quiz.exp: FINANCIAL_STAGE_0_PRE_INIT runs before Stage 1. Rules here set override_exclude_flag=True, causing Stage 1 to mark the candidate as 'excluded'. Excluded records are silently dropped — no fact record, no error group. Used for channel filtering, duplicate suppression, and pre-validation.

modules.title: Performance Monitoring & Incident Response
modules.desc: Monitor system health, analyze logs, and execute incident response procedures.

lessons.title: System health monitoring
lessons.content:
Key metrics to monitor:

CPU and memory utilization by microservice — baseline each service's normal range and set alerts for when it exceeds 80%.

API response time — increasing response times indicate load issues.

Nightly cycle duration — a cycle that normally completes in 2 hours but starts taking 4 hours is a warning sign.

Queue depth — unusually large queues may indicate a processing issue.

Integration feed status — confirm each feed delivered its expected volume.

lessons.title: Log analysis for incident diagnosis
lessons.content:
Log types:

Application logs — record system events, processing steps, and errors from each microservice.

Integration logs — record what data was received from each feed, when, and how many records were processed or rejected.

Access logs — record who logged in, when, and what they accessed.

Change logs — record all configuration changes and administrative actions.

lessons.title: Incident classification and escalation
lessons.content:
P1 (Critical) — Production outage or data corruption. Nightly cycle failure blocking financial reporting. 24/7 response.

P2 (High) — Significant functionality degraded. Multiple feeds failing. Response within 2 business hours.

P3 (Medium) — Isolated issues with a workaround. Response within 1 business day.

P4 (Low) — Minor issues with no operational impact. Response within 3-5 business days.

lessons.title: The nightly cycle and backup procedures
lessons.content:
A failed nightly cycle means AUM figures haven't updated, new trades haven't been attributed, and compensation calculations may be delayed.

Monitoring steps:
1. Confirm the cycle began at its scheduled time
2. Monitor progress — are individual job steps completing on time?
3. Confirm completion within the expected window
4. Verify the expected volume of records was promoted

Backup procedures: confirm backup completion daily and test recovery procedures quarterly. A backup you've never tested is a backup you don't actually have.

quiz.q: The system health dashboard shows memory utilization at 94% for the financial processing service. What do you do?
opts: Ignore it — high memory is normal
Investigate immediately: check running jobs, review logs for memory leaks, and escalate if approaching 100%
Restart the entire platform
Reduce the number of user accounts to free memory
quiz.exp: 94% memory is a warning threshold. Early intervention prevents outages.

quiz.q: An integration feed silently stopped sending data for 48 hours with no error in the queue. How do you detect this?
opts: Users would have reported it immediately
Proactive monitoring — volume-based alerts fire when a feed delivers zero records in an unexpected period
Silent failures are impossible to detect
Check the feed manually every hour
quiz.exp: Volume-based monitoring alerts catch silent failures before they impact reporting.

quiz.q: A P1 incident is declared at 2am — the nightly cycle failed. What is your response?
opts: Wait until business hours
Follow the documented incident runbook: assess scope, notify escalation contacts, attempt remediation, document all actions with timestamps
Restart the server and hope for the best
Manually enter missing data from source files
quiz.exp: Documented runbooks exist for 2am situations. Assess, notify, remediate, document.

modules.title: Audit Readiness & Compliance Management
modules.desc: Generate audit evidence, respond to auditors, and maintain SOX compliance posture.

lessons.title: What auditors look for in Synfinii
lessons.content:
During a SOX audit, IT auditors review three areas:

1. Change Management — can you demonstrate every Production change was tested in UAT and formally approved?

2. Access Control — can you demonstrate only authorized users have appropriate access, reviewed periodically?

3. System Availability — can you demonstrate the system met uptime commitments and incidents were handled according to documented procedures?

lessons.title: Generating an audit evidence package
lessons.content:
Access control evidence:
— User list with current roles, last login, and provisioning dates
— Records of access reviews with manager sign-offs
— Records of accounts deactivated when employees left or changed roles

Change management evidence:
— Log of all configuration changes during the period
— UAT test records for each Production change
— Approval documentation

System availability evidence:
— Uptime metrics, incident log, nightly cycle completion records

lessons.title: Responding to auditor requests
lessons.content:
Respond promptly — 24-48 hour turnaround signals control maturity.

Provide what was asked, not more — over-production can introduce confusion.

Never modify evidence — do not edit logs, backdate documentation, or adjust records. This is fraud.

Escalate unusual requests — if an auditor asks for access beyond the scope of your normal evidence package, escalate to management and legal before providing.

lessons.title: Maintaining ongoing compliance posture
lessons.content:
Monthly: review and update the user access list. Confirm all configuration changes are documented. Verify backup completion.

Quarterly: conduct formal access reviews with manager sign-offs. Review and update the Platform Playbook. Test the incident response runbook.

Annually: full review of RBAC role definitions. Full review of all active integration feeds. Review of rule engine configurations for any legacy rules still active.

quiz.q: An auditor requests evidence that no unauthorized changes were made to user access in Q3. What do you provide?
opts: A list of all current users
The access change logs for Q3 — showing every provisioning action, who performed it, when, and what approval was obtained
A report of all trades processed in Q3
The Data Steward's exception resolution history
quiz.exp: Access change logs show the full audit trail of every account creation, role change, and deactivation.

quiz.q: What is an 'audit evidence package' and when should you be able to produce one?
opts: A quarterly report submitted to regulators automatically
A compiled set of logs and documentation that proves controls operated effectively — producible on demand at any time
A document created only when an audit is formally announced
A backup of the entire database
quiz.exp: Audit readiness means producing evidence at any time — not just during audit season.

quiz.q: During an audit, the auditor finds a Production change with no corresponding UAT test record. What is this classified as?
opts: A minor administrative oversight
A Change Management control failure — a formal audit finding that requires remediation
Normal — small changes don't need UAT documentation
Only a problem if the change caused an error
quiz.exp: Any Production change without documented UAT testing is a Change Management control failure under SOX ITGCs.
